title: Local startups are quietly building Pakistan's next wave of AI tools
dek: A handful of small teams in Lahore and Karachi are shipping AI products for local businesses — skipping the hype cycle and going straight for problems no one else is solving.
author: Sara Khalid

body.text: While most of the attention in AI right now goes to large language models built by well-funded labs abroad, a quieter shift is happening closer to home. Small teams across Lahore and Karachi are building narrow, practical AI tools aimed at local business problems — inventory forecasting for retailers, Urdu-language customer support bots, and automated bookkeeping for small shops.

None of these products are trying to compete with the big foundation model providers. Instead, they wrap existing models with workflows built specifically for how local businesses actually operate — which, founders say, is where the real opportunity is.

"Nobody wants a chatbot. They want their end-of-day sales tallied automatically," said one founder building for retail shops in Lahore. That kind of focus, on unglamorous but recurring problems, is what's letting these teams find paying customers fast — often before they've raised any outside funding at all.

The trend also reflects a broader pattern: as AI infrastructure becomes cheaper and more accessible, the advantage shifts from who has the biggest model to who understands the problem best. For local teams with deep context on how small businesses in Pakistan actually run, that's a real edge.

It's still early. Most of these products serve a few dozen customers, not thousands. But the founders building them aren't chasing scale yet — they're chasing product-market fit in a market that's been mostly ignored by outside players.

metaDescription: A handful of small teams in Lahore and Karachi are shipping AI products for local businesses — skipping the hype cycle and going straight for problems no one el

title: Why on-device AI models are getting good enough to matter
dek: Smaller models running locally on phones are closing the gap with cloud AI — and that changes who can afford to build with it.
author: Omar Iqbal

body.text: For years, running a useful AI model meant sending data to a server somewhere and waiting for a response. That's starting to change. A new generation of compact models — small enough to run directly on a phone — are now capable enough to handle real tasks: transcription, translation, basic image editing, and simple assistants, all without an internet connection.

The shift matters most for cost. Cloud inference isn't free, and for apps with heavy usage, those costs add up fast. Running smaller tasks on-device removes that cost entirely, while also cutting latency and improving privacy since data never leaves the phone.

It's not a full replacement for large cloud models — on-device models still fall short on complex reasoning or long-context tasks. But for a growing list of everyday features, they're already good enough, and that's opening the door for smaller teams to build AI products without a large cloud budget.

metaDescription: Smaller models running locally on phones are closing the gap with cloud AI — and that changes who can afford to build with it.

title: A fintech founder's honest take on raising a seed round in 2026
dek: Investors want traction before conversation. One founder walks through what actually worked, and what wasted three months.
author: Ayesha Farooq

body.text: Raising a seed round has quietly gotten harder over the past two years, and fintech founders are feeling it more than most. Investors that once funded a strong team and a compelling story now want to see real transaction volume before they'll take a second meeting.

One founder, who closed a modest seed round earlier this year after nearly six months of fundraising, shared what actually moved the needle: not the pitch deck, but a simple dashboard showing weekly transaction growth that she updated and shared with investors every Friday — whether they asked for it or not.

"The deck got me the first meeting. The weekly numbers got me the check," she said. She also warned against a common mistake: spending too much time perfecting a narrative before there's enough real usage data to back it up. In her experience, three months were lost pitching an idea that wasn't yet proven, time that could have gone into getting more users instead.

metaDescription: Investors want traction before conversation. One founder walks through what actually worked, and what wasted three months.

title: The quiet rise of browser-based phishing kits
dek: Attackers are moving away from email and into the browser itself, using fake extensions to harvest logins in real time.
author: Hamza Rauf

body.text: Email-based phishing is still common, but security researchers are increasingly flagging a different attack surface: fake browser extensions designed to look like legitimate productivity tools. Once installed, these extensions can quietly capture login credentials as they're typed, without ever tripping a spam filter.

Because extensions request broad permissions by default, users often don't notice anything unusual until credentials are already compromised. Unlike email phishing, there's no suspicious link to hover over — the extension already has access once it's installed.

Security teams recommend restricting extension installs to a vetted allow-list, reviewing permissions regularly, and treating any extension that requests access to "all websites" with real scrutiny — even ones that appear to have genuine reviews.

metaDescription: Attackers are moving away from email and into the browser itself, using fake extensions to harvest logins in real time.

title: What it actually takes to fine-tune a model on a budget
dek: You don't need a data center. A breakdown of the real costs, tools, and trade-offs for small teams experimenting with fine-tuning.
author: Zainab Ali

body.text: Fine-tuning has a reputation for being expensive and complicated — something only well-funded teams with dedicated ML engineers can pull off. That's less true than it used to be. With parameter-efficient techniques and rented cloud GPUs billed by the hour, small teams can now fine-tune a mid-sized open model for a few hundred dollars.

The real cost isn't compute — it's data. Collecting and cleaning a few thousand good examples of the task you want the model to learn takes far more time than the training run itself. Teams that skip this step and fine-tune on messy or inconsistent data usually end up disappointed with the results.

For most small teams, the practical starting point isn't fine-tuning at all — it's a well-written prompt with a few examples. Fine-tuning becomes worth the cost once that approach plateaus and the task is narrow enough that a smaller, specialized model can outperform a general one.

metaDescription: You don't need a data center. A breakdown of the real costs, tools, and trade-offs for small teams experimenting with fine-tuning.

title: Why more founders are choosing to stay bootstrapped
dek: With funding harder to close, a growing number of early-stage teams are deliberately avoiding VC money — and it's working for some.
author: Bilal Hassan

body.text: As venture funding has tightened, a number of early-stage founders are opting out of the fundraising path altogether — building profitable, if slower-growing, businesses funded by customer revenue instead of investor capital.

The appeal is control. Bootstrapped founders don't answer to a board, don't need to hit growth targets set by someone else, and can make decisions on their own timeline. The trade-off is speed: without outside capital, growth is naturally capped by how much revenue the business can generate and reinvest.

It's not right for every business — capital-intensive ideas still need outside funding to get off the ground. But for software products with low overhead, bootstrapping is proving to be a realistic, and sometimes preferable, path.

metaDescription: With funding harder to close, a growing number of early-stage teams are deliberately avoiding VC money — and it's working for some.

title: Password managers aren't enough anymore. Here's what's next
dek: Passkeys are finally seeing real adoption. A look at how the shift is playing out across major platforms.
author: Noor Fatima

body.text: Passkeys — login credentials tied to your device instead of a typed password — have been available for a few years, but adoption is finally picking up as more major platforms make them the default option rather than an opt-in feature.

The appeal is straightforward: passkeys can't be phished the way passwords can, because there's no shared secret being typed into a page that could be fake. Even a well-crafted phishing site can't trick a passkey into authenticating on the wrong domain.

The remaining friction is cross-device use — passkeys created on one phone don't always sync smoothly to a laptop or another device. That gap is closing, but it's still the main reason many users stick with password managers as a backup, even as passkeys become more common.

metaDescription: Passkeys are finally seeing real adoption. A look at how the shift is playing out across major platforms.

title: We compared five budget phone cameras. One surprised us
dek: You don't need to spend flagship money for a genuinely good camera anymore. Here's what actually stood out in daylight, low light, and portraits.
author: Ayesha Farooq

body.text: Budget phones used to mean a clear compromise on camera quality. That gap has narrowed significantly, and in one case in this comparison, nearly closed entirely — a mid-range phone under a modest price point produced daylight photos that were difficult to distinguish from a phone costing nearly three times as much.

Low light remained the clearest differentiator across the group. Cheaper sensors still struggle to gather enough light without introducing visible noise, and software-based night modes only compensate so much before detail starts to smear.

Portrait mode edge detection — the software's ability to correctly blur the background around hair and fine detail — was the other major gap. The strongest budget option in this test handled it convincingly; two others struggled visibly around hair and glasses.

metaDescription: You don't need to spend flagship money for a genuinely good camera anymore. Here's what actually stood out in daylight, low light, and portraits.

title: Five productivity apps actually worth paying for in 2026
dek: Most productivity apps aren't worth a subscription. These five earned their place on our home screen.
author: Bilal Hassan

body.text: The productivity app market is crowded, and most of it is forgettable — reskinned to-do lists with a subscription wall. A small number of apps, though, have earned a genuine place in daily workflows by solving one problem clearly instead of trying to do everything.

What stood out across the apps that made this list wasn't feature count — it was restraint. Each one does a narrow job well: one focuses purely on quick capture of notes with no folders or tags to manage, another handles recurring task scheduling with almost no setup required.
The common thread: the best productivity tools right now are the ones that get out of the way fastest, not the ones with the longest feature list.

metaDescription: Most productivity apps aren't worth a subscription. These five earned their place on our home screen.

title: The Operator-to-VC Pipeline: Why Venture Capital is Snapping Up OpenAI Talent
dek: Venture firms are no longer just looking for financial wizards; they need partners who have navigated the scaling bottlenecks of the generative AI boom.
metaDescription: Venture capital firms are hiring OpenAI veterans like Ryan Beiermeister to cut through the AI hype and spot genuine technical innovation.
author: Circuit Desk

body.text: When venture capital firm Founders Fund announced former OpenAI executive Ryan Beiermeister as its newest partner, it represented more than just a standard executive hire. It highlighted a growing trend in Silicon Valley: the aggressive migration of elite artificial intelligence operators into the world of venture capital.

As the market becomes saturated with startups claiming to revolutionize industries through machine learning, venture firms are finding that traditional financial analysis is no longer enough. To separate genuine technical breakthroughs from clever marketing wrappers, investors need partners who have spent time inside the labs building the technology.
Cutting Through the AI Hype Cycle

For the past two years, early-stage AI investing has been characterized by astronomical valuations and rapid deal-making. However, as the initial excitement cools, the industry is entering a more sober phase. Investors are realizing that many AI startups lack defensible moats. Many are entirely dependent on third-party foundational models, leaving them vulnerable to platform risk.

This is where operators like Beiermeister come in. Having managed product and strategy at OpenAI during its most critical growth phase, she brings a deep understanding of what is actually difficult to build. An investor who understands model architecture, API dependency, and compute limitations can conduct a level of due diligence that generalist investors simply cannot match.
The Rise of the OpenAI 'Mafia'

The term 'PayPal Mafia' was coined to describe the alumni of the payments giant who went on to dominate Silicon Valley. Today, a similar phenomenon is occurring with OpenAI. Former employees are leaving the research lab to launch their own heavily funded startups, such as Anthropic and Covariant, or to join the investment firms funding them.

VC firms are eager to tap into this network. Hiring an OpenAI alumnus does not just bring technical expertise; it brings a highly valuable rolodex. In a hyper-competitive funding environment, personal connections to the next wave of departing researchers can be the difference between winning or missing out on a hot seed round.
What This Means for Founders

For startups pitching these firms, the bar for technical viability is rising. Founders can no longer rely on hand-wavy explanations of their technology when sitting across the table from a partner who helped scale ChatGPT. Pitch decks will face rigorous scrutiny on unit economics, data pipelines, and hardware efficiency.

Ultimately, this shift is healthy for the ecosystem. By bringing real-world operational experience into investment decisions, venture capital can direct funding toward genuine innovation rather than superficial applications. As AI matures into an infrastructure-heavy industry, the firms that succeed will be those that know how the technology actually works under the hood.

imageAlt: A sleek, modern venture capital office meeting room with floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking a city skyline.

title: Your Digital Twin Is Ready for the Boardroom: Google Workspace Pushes AI Avatars into the Office
dek: By integrating personalized AI avatars into Google Vids, the tech giant is betting that your next corporate update should be delivered by a digital clone.
metaDescription: Google is adding personalized AI avatars to Google Vids, allowing Workspace users to create digital clones. Here is what it means for office culture.
author: Circuit Desk

body.text: Recording a five-minute product demo or training video shouldn't require three hours of prep, a ring light, and ten failed takes. At least, that is the pitch behind the latest update to Google Vids, the search giant's enterprise video creation tool. By integrating Gemini-powered personalized avatars, Google is aiming to eliminate the friction of getting on camera by letting a digital version of you do the talking.

This update allows Workspace users to train an AI model on their own likeness and voice, generating a 'digital twin' that can read scripts, deliver presentations, and star in training materials. Powered by Google's Gemini Omni model, the system also streamlines the creation process by generating video storyboards, b-roll, and background music from simple text prompts and reference images.
The Rise of Asynchronous Office Video

For years, modern workplaces have championed asynchronous video tools like Loom as a cure for meeting fatigue. It is easier to send a quick walkthrough than to coordinate schedules across three time zones. Yet, actually recording those videos remains a chore. Many employees suffer from camera anxiety, and minor verbal slips often lead to endless re-recordings.

AI avatars shift the paradigm from performance to composition. Instead of performing, a user simply writes a script, uploads a slide deck, and lets their digital clone deliver the message. In theory, this democratizes high-quality video production inside the enterprise, allowing HR managers, product leads, and sales representatives to scale their communication without dedicating hours to video production.
Crossing the Corporate Uncanny Valley

While the efficiency gains are obvious, the introduction of digital twins to internal communications brings deep psychological and security questions. The human brain is highly sensitive to subtle artificiality in voice and facial movement. If a manager delivers bad news, such as a restructuring or a policy change, through an AI avatar, the lack of authentic human presence could alienate employees.

Furthermore, the rise of deepfakes raises the stakes for enterprise security. Google will need robust verification guardrails to ensure that employees cannot create unauthorized clones of executives or coworkers. If identity verification is compromised, the corporate digital twin could quickly become a powerful tool for sophisticated internal phishing attacks.
The Fight for the Enterprise AI Desktop

Google is far from the first company to explore this space. Startups like HeyGen, Synthesia, and Hour One have spent the last few years perfecting hyper-realistic AI avatars, gaining significant traction among marketing teams and educational content creators. However, Google holds a massive distribution advantage.

By embedding these capabilities directly into Google Workspace, Google bypasses the need for companies to sign new software-as-a-service contracts. Your AI avatar will live right alongside your Docs, Sheets, and Slides. As these digital clones become standard workplace tools, the line between authentic human presence and automated representation will continue to blur, changing how we define 'face time' in the digital age.

imageAlt: A close-up of a laptop screen displaying modern video editing and creation tools in an office setting.
